reference: GATE CS — Computer Organization and Architecture / Memory and I/O

map.condition: CPU time stolen per DMA transfer
Priority of simultaneous DMA vs interrupt
map.use: \text{DMA (bus request) has higher priority than interrupt}

map.condition: Programmed I/O vs interrupt-driven vs DMA speed

formulas.name: DMA CPU Overhead (cycle stealing)
formulas.whenToUse: When asked how many clock cycles or what fraction of CPU time is stolen by DMA per second

terms.means: clock cycles to initiate a DMA transfer
clock cycles to complete/acknowledge a DMA transfer
CPU clock period (1/f)
bytes per second delivered by the device
bytes moved per DMA burst

formulas.trap: Students forget to add BOTH initiate and complete cycles; some questions give cycles, others give time — convert consistently.

formulas.name: DMA Bus Cycle-Stealing Fraction
formulas.whenToUse: When the device transfers one word per bus cycle and you need the fraction of bus bandwidth consumed

terms.means: fraction of bus cycles stolen (0 to 1)
duration of one memory/bus cycle in seconds

formulas.trap: Use bus cycle time, not CPU clock period; they may differ especially when the memory bus runs at a different speed.

formulas.name: DMA Block Transfer Time
formulas.whenToUse: To find how long a DMA block transfer takes once initiated

terms.means: total bytes in the DMA block
I/O device throughput in bytes/s

formulas.trap: This is the I/O device time, not the CPU compute time — DMA frees the CPU during this window.

formulas.name: DMA vs Interrupt Priority Rule
formulas.latex: \text{Priority: DMA bus request} > \text{Interrupt request} > \text{Normal CPU execution}
formulas.plain: Priority: DMA bus request > Interrupt request > Normal CPU execution
formulas.whenToUse: When asked what a CPU does when a DMA bus request and an interrupt arrive simultaneously

terms.means: DMA controller requests the bus to steal a cycle
device signals CPU after completion

formulas.trap: Students confuse DMA priority with interrupt priority; DMA wins the bus, but the CPU still services the interrupt after the current instruction.

formulas.name: Disk Transfer Rate from Geometry
formulas.whenToUse: When disk geometry (diameter, density, RPM) is given and you must find how fast data reaches DMA/memory

terms.means: track diameter in cm (use innermost for max density track)
bits per cm (circumferential)
revolutions per minute

formulas.trap: Use the innermost track diameter for maximum storage density; outermost has lower density in CAV disks.

formulas.name: I/O Method Speed Hierarchy
formulas.plain: Programmed I/O < Interrupt-driven I/O < DMA  (in terms of CPU efficiency / effective throughput)
formulas.whenToUse: When comparing CPU utilization or data transfer speed across I/O methods

terms.means: CPU polls device in a busy-wait loop (most CPU waste)
CPU is interrupted by device when ready
controller moves data directly to memory; CPU minimally involved

formulas.trap: The question may ask about 'data transfer speed' (DMA wins) vs 'simplicity of implementation' (Programmed I/O wins) — read carefully.

formulas.name: Memory-Mapped vs I/O-Mapped I/O
formulas.latex: \text{Memory-mapped: I/O registers share address space} \Rightarrow \text{same instructions as memory}
formulas.plain: Memory-mapped I/O: same address space; no special IN/OUT instructions needed. I/O-mapped: separate port address space; uses IN/OUT instructions.
formulas.whenToUse: When asked which scheme is faster, uses more address space, or requires special instructions

terms.means: I/O ports in regular address space; load/store used; NOT necessarily faster for data transfer
separate port space; special IN/OUT instructions; frees address space

formulas.trap: Memory-mapped I/O is NOT faster for actual data transfer than I/O-mapped — the statement 'memory-mapped is usually faster' is FALSE.

formulas.name: Programmed I/O CPU Waste
formulas.latex: \text{CPU busy-waiting fraction} = \frac{\text{Polling cycles per transfer}}{\text{Total CPU cycles per second}}
formulas.whenToUse: When computing how much CPU time is wasted polling a slow device in programmed I/O

terms.means: CPU cycles spent checking device status per I/O unit
I/O device throughput rate

formulas.trap: Programmed I/O wastes CPU on every poll even if no data is ready; interrupt-driven I/O only uses CPU when data is truly available.

formulas.name: DMA Bus Request Handling
formulas.latex: \text{On DMA request: CPU releases bus} \Rightarrow \text{DMA transfers word} \Rightarrow \text{CPU resumes}
formulas.plain: DMA bus steal: CPU suspends after current memory operation, DMA uses bus for one cycle (or burst), then returns control to CPU.
formulas.whenToUse: When asked what happens when DMA requests the bus while CPU is executing

terms.means: DMA steals individual bus cycles between CPU accesses
DMA holds bus for entire block; CPU halted during transfer

formulas.trap: In cycle-steal mode the CPU is NOT halted for the whole transfer — only one cycle at a time is stolen.

formulas.name: Interrupt Response Sequence
formulas.latex: \text{CPU finishes current instruction} \rightarrow \text{saves state (PC, flags)} \rightarrow \text{jumps to ISR} \rightarrow \text{restores state, returns}
formulas.plain: 1. Complete current instruction  2. Save state (PC, PSW/flags)  3. Branch to ISR  4. Restore state  5. Resume
formulas.whenToUse: When asked what a CPU does upon receiving an interrupt or about the sequence of actions

terms.means: Interrupt Service Routine — the handler code
Program Counter — saved so execution can resume
Program Status Word / flags register

formulas.trap: CPU does NOT branch immediately mid-instruction; it waits until the current instruction completes before acknowledging the interrupt.

formulas.name: Vectored vs Non-Vectored Interrupt
formulas.latex: \text{Vectored: device supplies interrupt vector} \Rightarrow \text{CPU jumps to ISR directly}\\ \text{Non-vectored: fixed/polled location in memory}
formulas.plain: Vectored: device puts vector on bus -> CPU uses it as ISR address. Non-vectored: CPU polls or jumps to fixed location.
formulas.whenToUse: When asked how the CPU finds the ISR address or what 'vectored interrupt' means

terms.means: address (or index into IVT) supplied by the device
Interrupt Vector Table — maps vector number to ISR address

formulas.trap: In a vectored interrupt the branch address is NOT fixed in memory; the interrupting device provides it dynamically.

formulas.name: Interrupt Enable / Disable (IF Flag)
formulas.latex: \text{INTR processed only if Interrupt Enable (IE) flag is set}\\ \text{NMI bypasses IE flag}
formulas.plain: Maskable interrupt: processed only if IE flag = 1. Non-maskable interrupt (NMI): always processed regardless of IE flag.
formulas.whenToUse: When asked whether a CPU can be prevented from processing interrupts

terms.means: Interrupt Enable flag in PSW/flags register
Non-Maskable Interrupt — cannot be disabled by software
Maskable interrupt signal line

formulas.trap: A CPU CAN be set to not process maskable interrupts (by clearing IE); the statement 'CPU always processes interrupts' is FALSE.

formulas.name: When CPU Checks for Interrupts
formulas.latex: \text{CPU checks interrupt line at end of each instruction cycle (after completing current instruction)}
formulas.plain: Interrupt check occurs at end of each instruction fetch-decode-execute cycle, NOT mid-instruction.
formulas.whenToUse: When asked 'when does the CPU check for interrupts?' or about loop/instruction interruptibility

terms.means: one complete fetch-decode-execute of a single instruction

formulas.trap: Loop instructions (e.g., REP) CAN be interrupted between iterations — they are not atomic across all repetitions.

formulas.name: Daisy-Chain / Priority Interrupt
formulas.latex: \text{Daisy-chain priority: device closest to CPU gets highest priority}\\\text{Software polling: priority determined by poll order in ISR}
formulas.plain: Daisy-chain: hardware; priority = position in chain (first device = highest). Software poll: ISR checks devices in order; first checked = highest priority.
formulas.whenToUse: When asked how priority is resolved among multiple simultaneous interrupt requests

terms.means: hardware interrupt-acknowledge signal propagates through devices in series
ISR tests each device status register in sequence

formulas.trap: In daisy chain, the device closest to CPU wins, not the one with the 'most urgent' request — physical position determines priority.

formulas.name: Interrupt Latency
formulas.plain: Interrupt latency = time to finish current instruction + time to save CPU state + time to jump to ISR
formulas.whenToUse: When asked how long it takes from interrupt signal to first ISR instruction executing

terms.means: remaining cycles of the current instruction
cycles to push PC and PSW onto stack or into dedicated registers
cycles to load ISR address into PC

formulas.trap: Interrupt latency includes the current instruction completion time, which can be large for slow multiply/divide instructions.

formulas.name: DMA vs Interrupt-Driven I/O — When to Use
formulas.latex: \text{DMA: best for bulk/block data transfer (disk, network)}\\ \text{Interrupt: best for slow/infrequent devices (keyboard, mouse)}
formulas.plain: DMA: bulk block transfers (disk, NIC). Interrupt-driven: slow/infrequent character devices (keyboard). Programmed I/O: simple embedded / no OS.
formulas.whenToUse: When a question asks which I/O method is most appropriate for a given device type

terms.means: large contiguous data blocks at high bandwidth
generates I/O events rarely, polling would waste CPU cycles

formulas.trap: DMA overhead per byte is lower than interrupt-driven only for large blocks; for small/single-byte transfers interrupt-driven is often better.

formulas.name: Handshaking Protocol (Strobe / Ready-Acknowledge)
formulas.latex: \text{Strobe method: sender/receiver asserts strobe to signal data valid}\\ \text{Handshake: Ready} \to \text{data placed} \to \text{Acknowledge}
formulas.plain: Handshaking: 1. Source asserts Data_Valid (strobe). 2. Destination reads data. 3. Destination asserts Acknowledge. 4. Source de-asserts strobe.
formulas.whenToUse: When asked about synchronization between CPU/DMA and an I/O device without using interrupts

terms.means: single unidirectional signal indicating data is valid on bus
bidirectional ready/acknowledge ensures both sides are synchronized

formulas.trap: Strobe is unidirectional and cannot verify the receiver actually got the data; handshake adds acknowledge for reliability.
